topic: Christianity through a Study of the Gospel of Matthew / Mark / Luke
subject: religious-studies

items.correct: A short story from everyday life used by Jesus to teach a deeper spiritual truth, often about the Kingdom of God.
items.misconception: Parables are factual historical reports of events that actually happened.
items.random_wrong: A parable is a type of psalm sung in the Temple.
items.why: Students often read parables literally and miss the point: they are teaching stories whose meaning lies in the spiritual lesson, not in whether the events occurred.

items.q: Why are Matthew, Mark and Luke called the Synoptic Gospels?
items.correct: Because they share much of the same content, order and wording, so they can be 'seen together' (syn-optic) and compared side by side.
items.misconception: They are called synoptic because the same author wrote all three.
items.random_wrong: Because they were the only Gospels accepted by the Roman Empire.
items.why: Students confuse literary similarity with common authorship; the term describes the shared perspective and overlapping material, not a single writer.

items.q: What did Jesus teach about the Sabbath in the Synoptic Gospels?
items.correct: That 'the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' (Mark 2:27) — human need, such as healing, takes priority over rigid observance.
items.misconception: Jesus taught that Sabbath laws must never be broken for any reason.
items.random_wrong: Jesus abolished the idea of a day of rest entirely.
items.why: Students see Jesus' Sabbath conflicts as either total endorsement or total rejection of the law, missing his point that the Sabbath serves human wellbeing.

items.q: In the Gospels, what is a parable?
items.correct: A short everyday story teaching a spiritual truth
items.misconception: An eyewitness report of a miracle
items.random_wrong: A prayer recited in the synagogue
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A short everyday story teaching a spiritual truth. “An eyewitness report of a miracle” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is one of the Synoptic Gospels?
items.why: Correct answer: Mark. “John” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: According to the Synoptic Gospels, what did Peter declare at Caesarea Philippi?
items.random_wrong: That the Temple would be rebuilt
items.why: Correct answer: That Jesus is the Christ. “That Jesus should be baptised” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: At which event did Jesus share bread and wine with his disciples before his arrest?
items.random_wrong: The feeding of the five thousand
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Last Supper. “The Transfiguration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many disciples did Jesus call into his closest group?
items.why: The real figure is 12 disciples. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
